# EX09 - Data Analysis for Continous Improvement

<!-- This is a comment. Below, you'll see code for inserting an image. To make this image appear, update <custom-path>. To add an image, save it inside the imgs folder of this repository. -->
<img src="<custom-path>/static/imgs/logo.png" alt="Image of Comp110 rainbow logo. "  width="500"/>

## Introduction

Comp 110 could benefit from expanding there examples, there are many different majors that come into this class 
and are gaining experience through beginning their introductory knowledge into the world of programming. To base my point, I have anaylzed the different majors present within the course, the individuals experience coming into this course and the individuals interest into how computer science expands into the world past this course. This information was gained due to class-wide surveys released into 2 sections of a comp 110 course. 

I believe this data is valuable due to the main point of an introductory course being to inspire individuals to that field. Through focusing on different majors through a wide variety of examples used in class it can allow for individuals with no prior experience to not only understand the course but also realize that it's a valuable knowledge important for all future endeavors. 

## Analysis 1: Majors Present

My first analysis was focused around the different majors present within the course. Doing so I sorted the survey firstly into how many individuals of each major were present. 

That data showed there are 10 chemistry, 67 economics, 23 statistics and analytics, 39 psychology, 25 environmental science/studies, 39 business, 88 neuroscience, 21 mathematics, 11 information science, 40 data science, 4 media and journalism, 84 biology, 3 earth science, 1 biostatistics, 5 environmental health sciences, 1 nursing, 6 political science, 14 applied science, 5 computer science, 1 english, 1 medical anthropology, 9 exercise and sports science, 3 HPM, 1 peace war and defense, 4 advertising and PR, 1 health policy and managament, 1 human development and family science, 6 physics, 1 studio art, 4 biomedical engineering, 3 communications, 1 asian studies, 2 nutrition, 2 sports administration, 2 public policy, 1 geology, 1 management in society, 1 dramatic art, and 4 undecided or interdisciplinary studies majors. 

Clearly, a numerous amount of majors present within the course. Not all would fit on the graph comfortably so I separated them into groups before the graph below was constructed. Stem contains chemistry, neuroscience, mathematics, biology, biostatistics, physics, and biomedical engineering. Business contains economics, statistics and analytics, business, public policy, and management and society. Soft Science contains psychology, political science, peace war and defense, and asian studies. Environment contains environmental science/studies, earth science, environmental health studies, and geology. Computer contains information science, data science, applied science, and computer science. Media contains media and journalism, english, advertising and PR, communications, and sports administration. Health contains nursing, medical anthropology, exercise and sports science, HPM, health policy and management, human development and family science, and nutrition. Art contains studio art, and dramatic art. Undecidied contains undecided, N/A, and interdisciplinary studies. 

![Majors Graph]({{ site.baseurl }}/static/imgs/analysis1.png)

Those groups may be slightly biased by personal opinion however what was clear was that there was an overwhelming amount of majors specifically stem and business-based majors as compared to computer-based majors. This stands that examples outside of computer science may be more beneficial due to this large amount of different majors. 

## Analysis 2: Prior Experience

As we just analyzed there are many majors within this course. However, that doesn't yet eliminate the chance that even with the large amount of majors, there may be many individuals with experience who don't mind the computer-based examples used within the comp 110 course. Thus I used the survey data once more to sort out the individuals into categories used with the survey of experience spanning none to less than one month, 2-6 months, 7-12 months, 1-2 years, and over two years. 

![Experience Graph]({{ site.baseurl }}/static/imgs/analysis2.png)

Once again, a large overwhelming amount of individuals within the course displayed none to less than one month of coding experience. Even the second highest group, 2-6 months did not even reach half of the majority. 

## Analysis 3: Interest

My final analysis centered around the future of this course. If people could recognize computer science as important in other aspects even with examples only being computer science based. I analyzed survey data as people ranked from 1-7 on their interest in the connections between computer science and other fields. 

![Interest Graph]({{ site.baseurl }}/static/imgs/analysis3.png)

Thankfully, there was a large amount of people who ranked 5 in this displaying they do have interest and that examples in class did inspire them to think outside the computer science field, yet there were still numerous amounts of people within the 4 to 3 interest, which I believe through more broadened examples that tide could flip to 6 or 7. 

## Conclusion

I believe through the data shown in my previous analysis that it is important for the comp 110 course to broaden their examples to bigger majors. 

The first analysis that I had chosen to do was a depiction of each of the majors, separated into groups for legibility, and how the majority of the individuals within comp 110 are not majors that are centered around computer science. The overwhelming number of stem majors and business-based majors within the class as compared to computer science-based majors speaks to that many individuals coming into this course that would be familiar with examples outside of directly computer science. Based on the data alone, examples centered around stem, the majority of stem numbers being biology majors may be an essential set of examples to broaden the aspect of this course to the more diverse majors in the classroom. 

My second anaylsis broadened the analysis by not just focusing on majors but on experience, with the highest number of individuals in this course falling into the category of none to less than one month of experience with computer science and programming. I believe this also shows how vital expanding examples to a more diverse set of majors but also to maybe different life circumstances may make the examples easier to understand. The amount of individuals having even 2-6 months of experience is not even half the number of individuals with none to less than one month so making examples broader would assist the majority even if individuals with more coding experience may find them redundant. 

My third analysis as stated before was centered more around using these examples for students to build their idea of how computer science can be impactful in many areas of their life, and broaden their understanding of how computer science relates to many of their interests even if it's not their major. This data was much more averaged with the majority of individuals rating a 5 on how they are interested in how computer science connects to outside fields. This is a good sign as even with the current examples, students are still curious about how it interests, however I believe that we could increase the number of individuals to a higher interests through more outside interests as a number of students still gave it a 3 or 4. 

I think extensions of my idea could be centered around the third analysis, or the first. Within the first analysis, I believe it would be worth asking students if they believe comp 110, and intro to programming class should be a required course within their major. I think this would allow us a more personal look into how students view this course as vital to their major and then from their with using examples that coexist with their major that number could be higher. The third analysis could use more information on if they think computer science will involve their future goals and careers. 

However, with the change of examples, if focused to heavily to one major specifically, like I previously stated with the enormous amount of stem majors centered within this course, it may once again become a factor that it leans to heavily to one major and overlooks other groups of majors within this course.
